data.title: How Leveraging Big Data And AI Can Help Grow Your Portfolio
data.description:
What Is The Systematic Approach To Investing?

Systematic investing is an active approach to investing that emphasizes data-driven insights, scientific testing of investment ideas, and advanced computer modelling techniques to construct portfolios.

This process includes a comprehensive examination of empirical evidence by seasoned investment experts — testing different combinations of variables and comparing the results to known outcomes. This ability to validate insights means portfolio decisions are firmly evidence-based and not dependent on human conviction alone. Today’s data rich environment — data creation and storage has increased by a factor of 100 over the past 15 years1 — gives power to systematic managers who use large language models (LLMs) and machine learning algorithms to extract signals from a sea of data.

Signal boom

The increase in investment signals used by BlackRock Systematic

There are tens of thousands of data inputs, which can help lead to investment signals. For example, looking across online job postings as COVID-19 lockdowns eased, BlackRock’s systematic team found that demand for dog walkers was a significant data point. This data showed real-time movement toward reopening on a state-by-state level, reflecting a wider availability of vaccines and return to office mandates. This is one example of how we use alternative data sets — in this case, job postings for dog walkers — to uncover timely insights on the economy and companies.

Number of dog-walking postings

To be clear, having expert investors at every step of the process is essential. Systematic investing combines human expertise with technology to bring together the art and the science of investing. We believe the systematic principles of investing at scale with big data, technology and human insight can serve many different investment goals, including seeking alpha — or returns in excess of a broad benchmark — through our exchange traded funds (ETFs).

Outperformance, Income And Other Uses Of Systematic Investing

The consistent outperformance targeted by our systematic ETFs can help boost returns while also reducing overall risk and volatility — desirable outcomes in any market cycle, and especially valuable in today’s environment of heightened volatility.

Here are examples of actively managed iShares ETFs that leverage BlackRock’s systematic platform.

  • The iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO) seeks long-term capital appreciation by dynamically rotating exposure to U.S. market themes. These themes can include both long-term mega forces and shorter-term trends, allowing investors to pursue alpha opportunities as markets evolve.
  • The iShares U.S. Tech Independence Focused ETF (IETC) seeks to capture long-term growth opportunities using alternative data to identify companies across the technology landscape with a greater proportion of capabilities, revenues, and production derived in the U.S. and select global markets.
  • The iShares U.S. Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF) seeks to outperform the broad U.S. market by tactically allocating across factors based on real-time macroeconomic and market insights. DYNF dynamically selects stocks based on their exposure to specific factors — such as quality, value, momentum, growth, or low volatility — that the portfolio managers believe will do best based on forward-looking insights.
  • The iShares Advantage Large Cap Income ETF (BALI) uses the systematic team’s proprietary dividend rotation model to invest in a basket of large-cap stocks. The strategy seeks to enhance income by writing monthly call options on the S&P 500 Index while maintaining upside market participation.

Conclusion

Systematic investing has a rich history of evolution. At its core, it combines rigorous thinking and economic intuition with advanced forecasting techniques - including statistical analysis, economic modeling, and now, machine learning and artificial intelligence. These methods are applied to an ever-expanding universe of data sources. In today’s digital age that data goes beyond traditional financial information to capture sources like news stories, web traffic, social media sentiment, and consumer geolocation patterns.

We see a world of opportunity in systematic investing. Combining human insight with the power of AI and big data brings new levels of precision and efficiency to the investment process - helping uncover actionable insights and drive better portfolio outcomes.

data.title: What's Driving Bonds And Where Might They Be Headed?
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit and Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

With geopolitical uncertainties influencing credit markets, we discuss the various market drivers and where we think bonds may be headed in the coming months.

Craig: There’s been a lot of speculation about a buyer’s strike on US Treasuries, bonds, and equities. What’s your take?

Matt: We're not seeing a buyer strike, or even a buyer's pause. We're just seeing a little bit more competition. Yields are higher in a lot of different places, particularly in Japan, so they’re competing with US assets. That’s opposed to the last several years where they were the only game in town because of negative yielding debt around the globe. Also, there are some tax policies in the Trump administration’s “Big Beautiful Bill” that have the potential to deter foreigner investors. Also, there was an initial shock from tariffs and concern around volatility. There’s still uncertainty, but the market got a better feel for where Trump may be headed, and overall, there's still a huge demand for US Treasurys and fixed income.2

Craig: What's your thinking about the US Federal Reserve (Fed) cutting rates?

Matt: The market is still pricing in two cuts, but I think the feeling has increasingly been that we might not see any cuts this year. We’ve seen better-than-expected labor market statistics and investors are still concerned about inflation given the tariff uncertainty. If the Fed doesn’t cut rates this year, it generally will mean that the US economy has been better than people have been fearing. That's starting to look like it might be a possibility. On the other hand, if we were to go into a recession near term, the Fed could quickly cut three or four times to kick-start the economy. Overall, our opinion is that the economy is actually better than people had feared. We’ve now had three recessions predicted in just the last three years, and zero of them have played out.

Craig: What are your thoughts about positioning on the yield curve?

Matt: We had a curve steepening, which means we were long on the front end of the curve while being short on the long end of the curve.3 That helped performance. I think it makes sense to still be slightly overweight in the front end based on where we are now. As I said, we do think the economy is better than most people fear. However, if we are wrong and there is a recession, we have an overweight in credit assets. Credit spreads would widen in that scenario, but the curve positioning could somewhat offset that move if the Fed reduced rates. At the same time, we are cautious at the long end because the budget deficit is not going to get any better anytime soon.

Craig: What are your thoughts about bond returns for the rest of the year?

Matt: Annualized returns for fixed income since Q4, 2024 have been around 5.5-6%.4 But it hasn't felt good, in our view, because there are concerns around the budget deficit, inflation, and whether the Fed will cut or not. It's been volatile and there’s been a lot of uncertainty.

But you still have attractive yields, and that’s what matters most. The yield on the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index coming into the year was 5.3%. I think the yield on the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was about 4.9%. Those are the highest they've been in over 15 years.5 So a good way to think about bonds in our view is that you've gotten a better-than-expected return this year and your yield is still good, so you haven't missed the trade.

Craig: Is the market being too optimistic from a credit spread perspective? How do you think about the next leg of the corporate bond universe?

Matt: It's certainly an unloved bond market right now from a credit standpoint, in our view. I think a lot of investors wish there was more of a credit spread because there’s still uncertainty. However, yields are what's driving inflows, and it’s driving buyers to say “I can buy a high quality single A credit for 10 years at 5.5%. Why would I not want to do that?” We wish spreads were wider, even at their worst, we’re looking at them and saying, “well, they're not pricing in a recession. What happens if it occurs?” Overall, we believe the worst is behind us from a volatility standpoint, which generally means spreads should have a room to run from here.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P.. Year-end Yield to Worst on the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was 4.91%. In a flat environment you would expect the index to produce 2.04% year to date returns as of May 31 on a steady run rate; in actuality the index returned 2.45% as of May 31.
  2. Source: Bloomberg “Investors Snap Up Five-Year Treasuries in Show of Solid Demand” published May 25, 2025.
  3. A curve steepener is a trading strategy where an investor aims to profit from an increase in the yield difference between short and long-term bonds. If the yield curve steepens, it means the difference between long-term and short-term yields is increasing. This can happen because long-term yields rise more than short-term yields, or short-term yields fall more than long-term yields. "Long front end" means buying short-term bonds. If interest rates fall, bond prices rise, so you profit if short-term rates fall. "Short back end" means selling (or shorting) long-term bonds. If interest rates rise, bond prices fall, so you profit if long-term rates rise less than short-term rates.
  4. Source: Bloomberg L.P., Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index and Bloomberg US Corporate Total Return Index returns as of March 31st, 2025.
  5. Source: Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index Yield to Worst return was 4.91 as of 12/31/2024, Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index Yield to Worst was 5.33% as of 12/31/2024.

Important information

Image: Roberto Moiola / Sysaworld / Getty

Most references are US-centric and may not apply to Canada. All data is in USD unless otherwise stated.

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes USD denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility and financial issuers.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment-grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg FEDL01 Index represents the average interest rate at which commercial banks borrow and lend excess reserves to each other overnight. It's a key indicator of the cost of borrowing for banks and is heavily influenced by the Federal Reserve's target rate. The Federal Reserve uses this rate as a primary tool to implement monetary policy and manage the overall economy. The Bloomberg USGG2YR Index represents the yield received for investing in a US government issued treasury security with a maturity of 2 years. It is a key indicator of the shorter end of the yield curve and is considered important for understanding the overall US economy. The Bloomberg USGG30YR Index represents the U.S. Generic 30-year Treasury Yield. This index provides a historical rate for the 30-year U.S. Treasury, derived from market data, as opposed to the mathematically calculated "par-yield" provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

The yield curve plots interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of June 6, 2025 and are based on market conditions as of May 31, 2025. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals and are subject to change without notice.

These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

data.title: 2 Hidden Stocks Riding One Of The Biggest Booms Of Our Lifetime
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I wrote an article providing an update on my "Big Picture" research framework, which is the foundation of almost every financial decision I make, both on and off the stock market.

As we can see below, it's based on three major pillars:

  • Company-specific factors (this used to be "supply chains")
  • Economic Fundamentals (Fmr. "macro")
  • External Risks & Opportunities (Fmr. "politics")

Usually, I use this framework to discuss dividend stocks. In this article, I'll discuss two non-dividend-paying stocks. While this may potentially upset some of my income-focused followers, I can say I am NOT pivoting away from my core strategy, which is to invest every penny of spare cash in dividend (growth) stocks.

The goal of this article is to shed more light on one of the biggest economic trends at the moment, which will impact every single investor, regardless of age, strategy, and wealth status.

On top of that, I'm using it to expand my coverage, as I want to provide even more food for thought for an even bigger audience.

So, while we'll get back to income stocks tomorrow, this is the kind of article/content/info that I consider to be critical for everyone, as it allows us to assess our existing positions and make educated judgments about new investments and potential investment opportunities.

So, without further ado, let's jump right in!

Nuclear, It's Happening

I was close to turning 16 when the Fukushima nuclear accident happened in March 2011. Back then, I just started trading the markets (with very little knowledge and a huge ego), and curiously followed the reaction of market participants and politicians.

It was a tricky time, as Europe was just in the midst of a sovereign debt crisis, which occurred just two years after the Great Financial Crisis. Needless to say, most people were nervous. The first major nuclear accident since Chernobyl made this even worse, as it caused major economies to rethink their energy policies. This included Germany, which decided to say goodbye to the cleanest and most dense form of energy known to mankind.

Coupled with the nation's push into renewables, this turned out to be one of the biggest mistakes in its modern history, as it made Europe's biggest industrial nation prone to disruption, as the massive surge in energy inflation after the pandemic shows. After more than a decade of basically zero energy inflation, the absence of reliable energy caused its economic foundation to crumble, triggering a wave of bankruptcies and core economic trouble.

Now, nuclear is making a comeback, as even Germany has realized it may have made a mistake in light of the global boom in power demand.

With that said, nuclear energy has been a topic I have discussed for years. At the risk of sounding cocky, I think we have been on this trend before it became mainstream.

However, it is becoming such a big trend that it requires even more attention.

After all, in the current surge for power to support global electrification and next-gen technologies like advanced artificial intelligence, we need reliable and affordable energy. Nuclear energy isn't just dense, but it is also reliable, as nuclear power plants produce maximum power more than 92% of the time (the so-called capacity factor), according to EIA data.

According to Goldman Sachs' latest report, nuclear power is expected to see a rise from 378 gigawatts to 575 gigawatts by 2040, with an increasing number of countries pledging to triple nuclear power capacity by 2050.

Although the nuclear buildout continued after 2011, the share of nuclear power dropped to just 9% of the global power supply. That's down from the all-time high peak of 17% in the 1980s.

Increasing demand and regulatory support, such as President Trump's executive order aimed at reducing regulatory and cost barriers for new construction, create favorable conditions for new development.

While nuclear waste is still an issue, benefits like unmatched energy density, reliability, and no carbon emissions make the power superior and a great addition to natural gas reactors, which I am also extremely bullish on.

It also helps that lower regulatory hurdles and massive investment flows support technological advancements, creating new tailwinds like the future adoption of small modular reactors ("SMR").

New technologies are also set to boost nuclear power generation capacity. As investment in nuclear energy has started to pick up globally, countries are allocating money not only to large nuclear reactors but also to newer variants such as small modular reactors (SMRs). SMRs are a fraction of the size of a larger nuclear power plant, and they’re comprised of standardized components that can be mass-produced in traditional facilities and shipped to the site, unlike traditional reactors which are built on-site with larger components. SMRs typically have a lower power generation capacity (between 20-300 megawatts, compared to around 1GW for traditional nuclear reactors). - Goldman Sachs (emphasis added)

Although it will likely take until the late 2030s for SMRs to become mainstream, which is why natural gas is so critical, we are truly looking at one of the most favorable periods for nuclear energy adoption and innovation.

In fact, demand will be so high that the uranium supply is unlikely to keep up. That's why alternatives will remain critical and won't get replaced by nuclear energy for many more decades.

On the other hand, uranium production is set to increase from its current 80,000 tons to nearly 95,000 tons in 2030, before eventually declining to around 60,000 tons by 2045. In aggregate, the team is forecasting a uranium supply deficit of roughly 17,500 tons by 2030. “We anticipate this deficit to grow to roughly 100,000 tons by 2045 as new reactors come online,” write Lee and Davenport. - Goldman Sachs (emphasis added)

That's why I have been bullish on Cameco Corp. (CCJ) for many years, Canada's largest uranium miner and one of the biggest producers of nuclear fuel for Western companies. This has made it a beneficiary of rising nuclear power demand and increasing geopolitical risks.

Since my first article on the stock in November 2022, the company's stock price has nearly tripled, making it one of my best alternative energy tips.

However, while I remain bullish on this company, the two picks I am primarily discussing in this article are lesser-known companies I wanted to bring to everyone's attention.

Talen Energy Corp. (TLN) - Top-Tier Energy Infrastructure

Talen is a leading independent power producer and infrastructure company that owns and operates roughly 10.7 gigawatts of power infrastructure in the United States, including 2.2 gigawatts of nuclear power and a major dispatchable generation fleet.

It produces and sells electricity, capacity, and related services into wholesale markets, with most of its assets in the Mid-Atlantic region and Montana.

Related to the nuclear energy boom and data center demand, Talen is also powering what it calls the "digital infrastructure revolution." It is in a great position to capture this significant growth opportunity, as data centers serving artificial intelligence rely on dependable and very reliable energy.

In the first quarter, the company saw a 3.5% increase in weather-adjusted power deliveries in the PJM operating region, driving 1.6 TWh of additional generation from its fossil fleet. Essentially, this supports the company's view that rising energy demand will increase the dispatch of its flexible fossil assets. It also supports my thesis that every molecule of energy is needed.

Because of these qualities, the company is working closely with hyperscalers, which are some of the market's biggest companies. This includes a deal with Amazon (AMZN), to serve data center load, putting Talen in a position as a differentiated independent power producer.

Essentially, the deal shifts from a behind-the-meter to a front-of-the-meter (grid-connected) arrangement, with Talen as the retail provider for Amazon's Susquehanna data center and other Pennsylvania sites.

To add some more details, during this month's investor call, the company noted the agreement eliminates the need for FERC approval and addresses regulatory challenges faced after an ISA amendment rejection in 2024.

The transition to grid-connected delivery requires a minor electrical setup, expected during a 2026 refueling outage, with no slowdown in campus construction. Also, the contract expands the power purchase agreement ("PPA") to nearly 2 gigawatts (up from 960 MW) and unlocks the company's second nuclear unit that it previously held in reserve.

As one can imagine, this is very bullish for the company's financials, as it is expecting more than 50% growth in after-tax free cash flow per share by 2032, adding more than $8 to its 2026 guidance.

At full capacity, the deal is expected to generate $1.4 billion per year, with a total notional value of $18 billion over the full 17 years of the contracts. This shows just how HUGE this data center buildout is for companies further down the supply chain.

Between 2024 and 2032, the company aims for 20% annual growth in per-share post-tax free cash flow, one of the highest numbers across all companies on my radar. This, too, is a huge deal.

It will also allow the company to generate 70% of its income from long-term contracts and stable sources by 2032. In 2026, that number is expected to be just 40%. In other words, cash flow volatility will rapidly decline going forward.

So, what does this mean for its valuation?

Talen isn't super cheap.

However, it's far from overvalued, as we are dealing with a company capable of long-term double-digit annual free cash flow growth trading at less than 23x earnings. As a result, I expect the stock price to rise by 10-20% per year, supported by what I forecast to be a very rapid buildout of nuclear power capabilities and customers who look for direct solutions that Talen offers.

As such, Talen remains a Buy.

The same goes for the next pick.

Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU) - A Small Nuclear Gem You Probably Didn't Know

Centrus Energy is one of the companies I wish I had found out about earlier, as it's a fascinating player in the nuclear industry.

Headquartered in Bethesda, Maryland, the company is a leader in the supply of nuclear fuel and services, with a specialization in uranium enrichment for the nuclear power industry.

Even better, as the only U.S.-owned company currently enriching uranium with domestically developed technology, the company is a "mission-critical" player that supports areas like clean energy, energy security, and a wide range of other national security objectives.

Moreover, as we can see in the overview above, it services its customers through two major segments:

  • Low-Enriched Uranium ("LEU") provides nuclear fuel components like separative work units and enriched uranium products to utilities. It's also where the company got its ticker, which is why there is sometimes some confusion (I'll make sure to avoid it).

  • Technical Solutions offers engineering, manufacturing, and operational services to public and private sector clients. According to the company, since 1998, it has supplied more than 1,850 reactor years of fuel, which is the equivalent of more than 7 billion tons of coal.

Now, the company is working on pioneering the production of HALEU, which stands for High-Assay, Low-Enriched Uranium, which is a critical fuel for next-generation advanced reactors. The company's facility in Ohio is the first one in the U.S. using technology from the U.S. to boost production in almost 70 years.

In other words, on top of benefiting from the boom in nuclear energy demand, the company is also critical in America's effort to become less dependent on nations like Russia, which dominates the uranium supply through proxies like Kazakhstan.

The company also agrees with my thesis, citing various sources that see:

  • 40% growth in nuclear energy over the next three decades without new climate policies.
  • The need to triple nuclear power generation to achieve emission targets by 2050. While I doubt these targets will matter a lot when push comes to shove, it shows none of these targets can be achieved without nuclear power.
  • 90% of all nuclear reactors of the Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program operate on HALEU, the commodity Centrus is pioneering.

Total HALEU demand is expected to rise from 26 metric tons in 2025 to more than 610 metric tons by 2035. As Centrus is the only company with an NRC license for this material, it could benefit from a $6.2 billion HALEU market by 2035 like no other player.

It also helps that Centrus has a strong relationship with federal organizations, as federal investments in HALEU and LEU (the material) are expected to be a $3.4 billion catalyst, with national security demand outpacing global utility demand, which is something a lot of people seem to overlook.

During its 1Q25 earnings call, the company explained it has secured three DOE contracts for HALEU production, HALEU deconversion, and LEU production, which is supported by more than $3.4 billion in congressional appropriations, including $700 million from the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.

Essentially, these contracts position the company to benefit from significant federal investment aimed at restoring domestic nuclear fuel production.

It currently has a backlog of $3.8 billion.

Moreover:

  • It has a balance sheet with more than $650 million in unrestricted cash.
  • Its net income has grown by 29% per year since 2017.

The problem is putting a valuation on LEU. It's the kind of company analysts usually struggle to value, as many contracts are long-term and can be influenced by new policy changes. Every new contract could meaningfully boost the long-term growth trajectory.

Currently trading at a blended P/E ratio of 41x earnings, LEU isn't cheap. However, I do not consider it expensive, as it has a wide-moat business model, a critical position in America's national security, and a front seat in the nuclear boom.

While I cannot give a clear price target, I apply a Buy rating due to my bullish view on the industry, its business model, and the company's 41x multiple.

A Quick Note

Now, with all of that said, I need to reiterate that both of these stocks are volatile. They are a clear detour from the (dividend) companies I usually cover. The main goal was to discuss the massive opportunity in nuclear energy, different areas that benefit, and other things to keep in mind, including national security.

While I consider TLN and LEU to be two of the best players that most still don't know, investors need to be aware of their volatility, and I would not touch these companies without a long-term mindset.

Also, tomorrow, we're back to discussing dividend stocks, as I finally made good on another promise I made to some readers.

Takeaway

Nuclear energy is back, and it's bigger than ever.

After years of being sidelined by mostly Western nations since Fukushima, the world is waking up to the fact that we need reliable, dense, and clean power to fuel AI, electrification, and general economic growth.

One could say there is no way around nuclear energy.

In this article, I highlighted two non-dividend stocks, which are Talen Energy and Centrus Energy, which are poised to capitalize on this megatrend.

Talen's deal with Amazon underscores the critical role of nuclear in powering data centers, while Centrus is a critical player in America's push for energy independence through HALEU production.

Moreover, while both offer explosive growth potential, they come with volatility.

All things considered, whether you invest in these names or not, understanding the nuclear renaissance is key to navigating the next decade, which is why I will put a lot more emphasis on Big Picture developments.

data.title: Nike Looks Broken, But It Might Be A Bargain
data.description:
Over the long run, Nike, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) has been a great investment, and early investors became very rich. However, in the last few years, Nike did not only underperform the S&P 500, but investors actually did lose a lot of money. In late 2021, the stock price peaked, and since then, the stock has been in a constant decline.

In November 2024, I published my last article about Nike and I started to turn slightly bullish. I rated the stock as a “Buy” already, but I argued in my conclusion that we should still remain cautious:

At this point, I certainly must change my rating, as Nike is not a “Sell” anymore. I though long about whether Nike is now a “Buy” or if we should rate it as a “Hold” but in the end, Nike is trading about 20% below its intrinsic value and seems close to a strong support level, which makes it a cautious “Buy” in my opinion.

Of course, Nike is struggling right now, and the next few quarters will not be easy for a company that is depending on consumer sentiment. But in the long run, the wide economic moat will lead to high-growth rates. And this makes Nike a cautious “Buy” at this point.

And while I turned slightly bullish in November 2024, the stock continued to decline and lost almost 20% in value in the meantime. In April 2025, I started buying Nike for myself, although I still don’t know if Nike actually has hit its bottom. In the following article, I will explain why I think that Nike is already undervalued and a long-term buy. However, I will also argue why I think that Nike could decline a little bit further, and we might not have seen the bottom yet.

Business Struggling

When looking at the company’s last quarterly results, there is not much reason to be optimistic. On March 20, 2025, Nike reported third-quarter results for fiscal 2025, and although Nike did beat analysts’ estimates for revenue ($240 million higher) and earnings per share ($0.26 higher), the results were rather a disappointment when looking at year-over-year comparisons.

Revenue declined from $12,429 million in Q3/24 to $11,269 million in Q3/25 – resulting in a 9.3% year-over-year decline. Income before income taxes declined even by 39.9% year-over-year from $1,404 million in the same quarter last year to $844 million in this quarter. Diluted earnings per share also declined from $0.77 in Q3/24 to $0.54 in Q3/25 – resulting in a 29.9% year-over-year decline.

When looking at the results in more detail, we see that Nike is struggling in all geographic regions. But while revenue declined “only” 4% in North America and 10% in Europe, Middle East & Africa, it was especially Greater China that is still struggling. Revenue in the region declined from $2,084 million in Q3/24 to $1,733 million in Q3/25 – resulting in a 17% year-over-year decline.

When looking at the different categories, equipment (2% year-over-year decline) and apparel (3% year-over-year decline) were not performing too badly, while footwear was the major problem with revenue declining 12% year-over-year.

Growth Potential?

I mentioned several times that we should not just look at one single quarter as this is just one datapoint in time. However, it is not just the last quarter, which was disappointing for investors. Nike reported its fourth consecutive quarter of declining revenue.

When looking at the chart above, this is not the only time that Nike had to report declining revenue, declining EPS, or declining free cash flow. And management is expecting the business to continue struggling in the fourth quarter:

Looking ahead, we believe that the fourth quarter will reflect the largest impact from our Win Now actions and that the headwinds to revenue and gross margin will begin to moderate from there. We are also navigating through several external factors that create uncertainty in the current operating environment, including geopolitical dynamics, new tariffs, volatile foreign exchange rates, and tax regulations, as well as the impact of this uncertainty and other macro factors on consumer confidence.

Our fourth quarter guidance includes our best assessment of these factors based on the data we have available to us today. We expect Q4 revenues to be down in the mid-teens range, albeit at the low end. This includes several points of unfavorable shipment timing in North America, as well as 2 points of negative impact from foreign exchange headwinds. We expect Q4 gross margins to be down approximately 400 basis points to 500 basis points, including restructuring charges during the same period last year. We have included the estimated impact from newly implemented tariffs on imports from China and Mexico.

And when looking at analysts’ estimates for the coming quarters, we also see constantly lower estimates for earnings per share, which is never a good sign.

While analysts constantly lowered estimates for the years to come, earnings per share are still expected to grow with a CAGR of 16.11% (and even when comparing estimates to the peak in 2024, we still get a growth rate of 7.5% annually for the next decade).

And although management is clearly acknowledging that the business is struggling, it also seems to have a strategy to lead Nike back on the path of long-lasting and sustainable growth. Among the different strategies is the idea to make the brand distinctive again. During the last earnings call, management stated:

The next action is shaping our brand for distinction. This is about lining up storytelling power of NIKE to celebrate the passion and emotion of sport. In February, I attended the Super Bowl and the NBA All-Star weekend to see firsthand the kind of impact we make when we line up all of our advantages towards one moment.

Additionally, Nike is trying to diversify its product portfolio and broaden its product lineup. It is also trying to diversify the business to be less dependent on footwear sales, which are struggling right now and are still responsible for a huge part of total revenue. During the last earnings call, CEO Elliott Hill stated:

Shifting to products, we're beginning to drive a more diversified portfolio. It will take time to reach the volume to replace the handful of classic franchises we over-indexed on. But our approach is simple. Help consumers fall in love with something new from NIKE, and that something is not replacing one icon for another. It's about building a supporting cast of multiple styles, some that are gaining interaction with men and others with women's.

I remain confident in Nike’s ability to continue growing over the long run (with some struggles in the short run), but tariffs could remain a major problem for Nike. In May 2025, the company already announced that it will raise prices, and Nike is certainly one of the companies affected the most by tariffs on Chinese goods. Although the signs between China and the United States are rather pointing towards easement than rising tension right now, this could change quickly. And as I have written in different articles already, I think the frequent shifts in trade policy will lead to huge damage to the economy – not only in the United States.

Nike is probably one of the companies that will suffer the most in the next few quarters, but over the long run, the business will handle these challenges and continue growing at a high pace. And right now, share buybacks are also contributing to the bottom-line growth.

Share Buybacks and Dividend

When looking at how Nike is distributing its cash, we see that share buybacks always played a role for Nike and the company constantly bought back shares. However, in the last three years, the decrease in shares outstanding accelerated a little bit. The number of diluted shares outstanding declined from 1,526.5 million in Q3/24 to 1,480.6 million in Q3/25. This has resulted in a 3.0% decrease in shares outstanding in the last 12 months.

However, the acceleration is not stemming from Nike spending more cash on share buybacks but rather from the low stock price. Management is spending a similar amount on share buybacks as it did a few years ago, but it can repurchase more shares with the same amount of cash. In my opinion, Nike could even accelerate its share buybacks further and take advantage of Nike trading for one of the lowest valuation multiples in the last 10 years. We don’t have any information if Nike wants to accelerate its share buyback program, but with $11.8 billion already spent, the company has $6.2 billion remaining of the current $18 billion share buyback authorization.

Nike is also constantly increasing its dividend. The last time, it raised the dividend by 8.1% to a quarterly payment of $0.40 per share. The company has now increased dividends for 23 years in a row, and in the last five years, it increased the dividend with a CAGR of 10.45%. And with a stock price of $62 right now, we get a solid dividend yield of 2.6%.

The Stock Is Cheap

One of the strongest arguments we can make for Nike, at this point, is the valuation of the stock, particularly its relatively low multiples. At the time of writing, Nike is trading for 20 times earnings, which is clearly below the 10-year average P/E ratio (which was 37.19) and close to the 10-year low. When looking at the price-to-free-cash-flow ratio, the picture is similar. Right now, Nike is trading for 17 times free cash flow, which is also close to the 10-year low and clearly below the 10-year average of 40.63.

In my last article, I already argued that Nike is undervalued and trading below its intrinsic value. Now the stock is trading even 20% lower, and it should therefore not come as a surprise that the stock is clearly trading below its intrinsic value. Nevertheless, I will offer another discount cash flow calculation and provide a few different scenarios and more detail in this article.

As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate as this is the annual return I like to achieve at least. Additionally, I calculate with 1,481 million in diluted shares outstanding. Once again, I will calculate with the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $5,311 million, and in the last article, I calculated with 9% growth for the next 10 years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $83.35 for the stock, which is trading 25% below the intrinsic value right now.

In my previous calculation, I used a higher free cash flow as the basis (as Nike was reporting a higher trailing twelve months FCF). However, when looking at the free cash flow margin in the last four quarters, it was 11.1% and therefore much higher than the long-term average of 7.5%. And even when looking at the last 10 years, “only” 9.8% of revenue ended up as free cash flow. In my opinion, we should be cautious and not really calculate with a higher free cash flow.

But we can argue that 4% growth until perpetuity might be a bit too cautious. While such a terminal growth rate is in line with CFI’s guidance, I would argue that Nike is one of the companies that might be able to outpace the overall market for a longer time. These charts show a long-term performance of the U.S. stock market around 7% (exemplified by the S&P 500). Sometimes growth rates were lower, and sometimes they were a little higher. But even more important than the stock price performance is earnings per share growth. When looking at the last century, earnings per share also grew with a CAGR of 6% over the long run (the number also depends on what timeframe we are looking at).

And we can assume 9% for the next ten years as a reasonable growth rate. This is below the growth rate Nike could achieve in the past, and also below the growth rate analysts are expecting for the years to come. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $110.87, and the stock would clearly be a bargain.

Conclusion

Nike will report fourth quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2025 next week, but as management already lowered expectations during the last earnings call, the company could actually exceed analysts’ expectations for revenue and earnings per share once again. During the earnings call, we should especially pay attention to what impact tariffs have had on Nike’s business so far.

In the next few months, declining stock prices are a possibility, and the stock might set new 52-week lows again. However, over the long run, I think Nike is a good investment at this point, and investing in the stock at current prices will lead to above-average returns.

data.title: Millicom's Quiet Comeback
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past decade, Millicom International Cellular S.A. (TIGO) has transformed from a geographically scattered operator into a focused Latin American telecom company. This transformation is already bearing fruit. From 2022 to 2024, operating profit grew at a 24.8% CAGR despite modest revenue growth, while return on capital improved significantly. It now ranks among the top performers in its peer group regarding profitability and earnings momentum.

My valuation based on optimistic assumptions provided a 27% margin of safety at the current market price. However, the balance sheet remains stretched, with high leverage and a history of aggressive reinvestment. Its volatile tax rate also reflects the complexity of operating across diverse jurisdictions. As such, I would look for its price to drop by another 10% before buying.

A Decade Of Corporate Realignment

Today, Millicom operates as a focused telecommunications and digital services provider under the TIGO brand, exclusively in Latin America. It reports its business in three primary categories:

  • Mobile services. This also covered mobile financial services besides voice, SMS, data, and roaming. As can be seen from the right part of Chart 1, this is the biggest revenue contributor.
  • Fixed Services. This included broadband internet, cable/pay TV, and fixed voice services.
  • Other. This included handset and equipment sales, advertising, and other non-core revenues.

A decade ago, the company also had operations in Africa, but these were fully divested by 2022. At the same time, it doubled down on its Latin American markets, acquiring leading cable and mobile assets in Panama and Nicaragua. It also consolidated full ownership of its operation in Guatemala.

As such, today, its geographic focus is on Latin America, with both Colombia and Guatemala accounting for about half of the total revenue. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

Note to Chart 1: The 2020 segment revenue onwards was based on the divestment of Africa and the consolidation of the Guatemala and Honduras operations.

Millicom today is the product of a decade of deliberate corporate actions - acquisitions, divestments, and ownership consolidations - that reshaped the business both geographically and structurally.

These corporate exercises gave Millicom not only scale but also operational clarity.

  • Joint ventures were either exited or converted into fully controlled subsidiaries.
  • Network-sharing agreements and tower divestments further streamlined the business model.

Together with internal restructuring programs like Project Everest that reinforced cost discipline and efficiency, Millicom is today a leaner, regionally focused operator in a market with a single-digit growth rate.

“The telecom services market in Latin America is expected to reach a projected revenue of US$ 99,371.9 million by 2030. A compound annual growth rate of 4.4% is expected for the Latin America telecom services market from 2026 to 2030.” Grand View Research

“The broadband services market in Latin America is expected to reach a projected revenue of US$ 82,754.1 million by 2030. A compound annual growth rate of 6.7% is expected for the Latin America broadband services market from 2025 to 2030.” Grand View Research

Growth Drivers

Despite the positive market outlook, Millicom’s CAGR in revenue from 2022 to 2024 was subdued at 1.6%.

Looking at Chart 1, you can see that the revenue from 2020 onwards was significantly higher than that pre-2020. This was due to the consolidation of the Guatemala and Honduras operations.

  • The Guatemala consolidation was because the company acquired the remaining 45% of its stake in the Guatemala joint venture in 2022.
  • From a segment reporting perspective, the company included “the Honduras joint venture as if it were fully consolidated, as this reflects the way management reviews and uses internally reported information.”

As mentioned earlier, the market Millicom operates in is a single-digit one, and the company’s average annual organic growth rate over the past 3 years was only about 2%.

As such, while the outlook for organic growth post-2024 may be optimistic, driven by the following customer demand and operational tailwinds, I do not expect the company’s organic growth rate to beat those projected by the market research reports:

  • Q1 2025 saw strong momentum for mobile and home broadband return to growth after a weak 2024, with expectations for sustained customer acquisition.
  • Continued rollout of FTTH infrastructure in core markets.
  • Potential deployment of fixed-wireless 5G in areas with low-fiber economics.
  • Cloud, cybersecurity, and connectivity solutions under TIGO Business grew about 15% in 2024 and are expected to scale further.

Return Turnaround

Despite the marginal revenue growth from 2022 to 2024, operating profit grew at a 24.8% CAGR. This was driven by margin expansion with a gross profit margin in 2024 of 75.5% compared to the 73.2% in 2022.

At the same time, there was a significant reduction in the Selling, General and Administration [SGA] margin. This, in turn, led to a reduction in the fixed cost margin from 57% in 2022 to 52% in 2024. Refer to Chart 2.

The impact of these was a significant growth in the returns.

  • ROIC went from 3.1% in 2022 to 5.2% in 2024.
  • ROE went from 3.5% to 7.5% in 2024.

These improving returns augur well for shareholders as the historical average returns were lower than the current WACC of 7.0% and cost of equity of 8.3%. In other words, historically, shareholders’ value was not created.

Notes to Chart 2: a. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, R&D and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

b. The metrics in these Charts were based on the financial statements, where in the 2020 and 2021 performances, Guatemala and Honduras were not consolidated.

There is now an opportunity to create shareholders’ value moving forward. This is because the operating trends from 2022 to 2024 appear structurally driven and largely sustainable, due to:

  • Completion of the Project Everest, which delivered reduced fixed costs and streamlined operations. These are largely structural rather than one-off cuts, suggesting ongoing benefit to margins.
  • The gross profit margin increase was driven by a service revenue mix shift toward higher-margin postpaid mobile and B2B services. There were also improved customer lifecycle and retention metrics. These are tied to deliberate business model refinements, not temporary windfalls.

Of course, continued better performance will depend on reinvesting smartly for growth without re-inflating the cost base.

One of the better peer performers

I compared Millicom’s performance with some Latin American telcos. I also included Veon, which I covered for Seeking Alpha recently. Refer to Table 1.

Millicom is among the small players in terms of 2024 revenue. You can see from Table 1 that only one of the panel members had a positive growth rate over the past decade. But when you consider the 2022 to 2024 revenue growth, only Millicom and Veon delivered positive growth rates.

Notes to Table 1:

  • América Móvil (AMX). Based in Mexico, it's the largest telecom operator in Latin America under the Claro and Telcel brands.
  • Liberty Latin America (LILA). Focused on cable, broadband, and mobile services in the Caribbean and select LATAM countries.
  • Telefónica (TEF). This is a Spanish multinational still significant in Latin America, operating the Movistar brand across major LATAM markets.
  • VEON Ltd. (VEON) is a Nasdaq‑listed digital telecom operator serving approximately 160 million customers in emerging markets across Eastern Europe and South Asia.

I compared Millicom’s 2015 to 2024 performances with those of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 and 4.

Compared to peers, Millicom has demonstrated one of the most compelling peer performances over the long term, with a strong turnaround across key metrics. This sustained improvement across profitability, capital efficiency, and cash generation positions Millicom as a clear operational leader within its peer group by 2024.

  • Millicom’s return on capital had a U-shaped pattern, with the 2024 return being higher than that in 2015. Its 2024 return was among those in the upper ranges. More importantly, it ranked No. 2 in terms of improvement from 2022 to 2024.
  • I would rate Millicom’s EBIT margin performance as one of the better ones. It has improved so that by 2024, its EBIT margin was very close to that of the No. 1 ranking company.
  • Millicom had the best free cash flow margin in 2024. This was a significant improvement, as it was among the worst performers in 2015.
  • Millicent also ranked No. 2 when it came to improvements in the EPS from 2015 to 2024. Two of the panel members had lower EPS in 2024 compared to those in 2015.

Financial Position

I have concerns about Millicom's financial position.

Its main positive point was its cash flow position. Over the past decade, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year with a good cash flow conversion rate. During this period, it generated a total of USD 10.8 billion in cash flow from operations compared to its cumulative break-even profit after tax.

But this was outweighed by the following:

  • It did not have a good capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow was not enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions.

  • It has a high 213% Debt-to-Equity ratio as of the end of March 2025. However, this has come down from its 2021 high of 325%.
  • As of the end of March 2025, it had cash of about USD 534 million, equal to about 4% of its total assets.
  • It had an unsustainable Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) that averaged 103% over the past decade. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

Is Millicom fundamentally sound?

My findings showed that Millicom has emerged as a fundamentally sound telecom operator operationally, with a strong strategic focus, improving returns, and peer-leading efficiency.

  • Between 2022 and 2024, Millicom significantly improved EBIT margins and lowered fixed costs via Project Everest, indicating structural (not cyclical) margin gains.
  • Return on capital and ROE both showed strong upward trends. These are key signs of a sustainable turnaround, especially given its historically sub-cost of funds performance.
  • Despite its smaller size, Millicom’s 2024 performance outshone larger Latin American peers in terms of margin expansion, return improvement, and EPS growth.

However, its financial position is a weak link that must be addressed to fully realize long-term value creation. The picture that emerges is that this is an operationally strong but financially stretched company, but with momentum in the right direction.

Valuation

In my valuation, I have adopted the following picture:

  • The revenue would be the 2024 revenue growing at 3% in perpetuity. This is higher than the past 3 years' average organic growth rate of 2%.
  • The base contribution margin and fixed cost margin would be the 2023/24 average margins. This would improve proportionately to be 5% better in the terminal year. Note that the 2024 contribution margin was 4% higher than that in 2015.
  • The base capital efficiency would be the 2023/2024 average values.
  • The base reinvestment margin would be based on the past decade's average. This would be improved so that in the terminal year, the reinvestment rate would be governed by the fundamental growth equation.
  • The tax rate would be based on the 2024 tax charge of USD 284 million before tax charges/credits for prior years, deferred tax assets, and impairment. I estimated that this was about 39% of the 2024 PBT before unusual items of USD 731 million.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 47 per share compared to its market price (18 Jun 2025) of USD 37 per share. There is a 27% margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Millicom based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm [FCFF] model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost margins are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The reinvestment was based on the reinvestment margin.

Notes to Table 3:

a. No change

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed proportionate change

d. Assumed proportionate change

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “TIGO WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Valuation Risks

I believe that my assumptions are optimistic, looking at the historical trends vs those projected by the valuation model.

  • The projected free cash flow is much higher than that projected by the log normal trend line (shown in dotted line in the right part of Chart 5).
  • While I have projected a steeper revenue trend line compared to the past 3 years trend, the projected contribution margin appears reasonable.

I also found the following when comparing the values projected by the valuation model to the historical values:

  • 8% return on capital compared to the 2022 to 2024 average ROIC of 4%.
  • 26% EBIT margin compared to the 2022 to 2024 average EBIT margin of 18%.

The challenging issue here is the tax rate. In my valuation, I have assumed 39%. Millicom had a very volatile tax rate over the past decade, ranging from a 334% tax credit to a 242% tax rate, with a simple average tax rate of 45%.

The tax rate volatility was driven by a combination of structural factors (multi-jurisdictional operations), accounting adjustments (deferred taxes), and episodic events (transactions, audits). It reflects the complexity of operating across diverse and evolving tax jurisdictions in Latin America. As such I see the tax rate as a key valuation risk.

In summary, the combination of tax uncertainty, a stretched balance sheet, and optimistic valuation assumptions should all temper the margin of safety.

Conclusion

Millicom stands out as a compelling turnaround story in the Latin American telecom landscape. Over the past decade, it has undergone a strategic transformation:

  • It divested its African operations.
  • It consolidated ownership in key Latin American markets.
  • It also streamlined its operations through internal restructuring.

These efforts improved efficiency with EBIT margin, fixed cost margin, and ROIC all showing marked improvement between 2022 and 2024. Peer comparisons further highlight Millicom's relative outperformance.

However, I have concerns about its financial position. A persistently high debt-to-equity ratio and historically weak capital allocation decisions have stretched the balance sheet.

While operational momentum is positive, sustaining its shareholders' value creation will depend on disciplined reinvestment, balance sheet repair, and maintaining margin improvements.

From a valuation perspective, there appears to be a meaningful margin of safety at current prices, with intrinsic value estimated at USD 47 per share versus a market price of USD 37.

But this is based on optimistic assumptions. While they are not implausible given Millicom’s demonstrated improvements, you should remain mindful of risks, especially surrounding the company’s volatile tax rate.

In sum, Millicom is fundamentally sound operationally with a positive earnings trajectory. But given its financial position and tax exposure, I would want a higher margin of safety. As such, I would wait for the price to drop by at least another 10% before entering.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Endeavour Silver: Approaching Its Free Cash Flow Inflection Point
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground.

Q1 Production

Endeavour Silver (NYSE:EXK) released its Q1-25 results last month, reporting quarterly production of ~1.21 million ounces of silver and ~8,300 ounces of gold or ~1.9 million SEOs. This translated to an 18% decline in payable gold and silver production year-over-year, impacted by lower silver grades and recoveries and a decline in throughput at Guanaceví (Mexico) in addition to lower gold grades at Bolañitos. The result was that Endeavour Silver was one of the few companies to see declining revenue year-over-year despite record gold prices and a much higher silver price ($31.99/oz vs. $23.47/oz), and this was despite higher ounces sold than produced in the period.

Starting with Guanaceví, production came in at ~1.02 million ounces of silver and ~4,000 ounces of gold at $26.50/oz AISC, down from ~1.19 million ounces of silver and ~4,100 ounces of gold in the year-ago period. As noted, lower production was impacted by a decline in throughput (~102,400 tonnes) and lower grades (348 G/T of silver vs. 402 G/T of silver) and the asset has seen a consistent decline in reserve tonnes over the past few years.

In fact, reserve tonnes have slid to just ~459,000 tonnes (year-end 2020: ~1.09 million tonnes), with the current reserve base supporting less than two years of mine life assuming a 1,000 TPD throughput rate. This is not ideal (assuming we don't see meaningful reserve additions) given that this is a ~5+ million ounce per annum asset on a silver-equivalent basis that's set to offset a sizable chunk of the growth coming from Terronera (~7.0 million SEOs per annum over LOM) when it heads offline.

As for cost performance in the period at Guanaceví, AISC jumped 21% year-over-year despite lower sustaining capital spend and will remain elevated given what's likely to be increased capex through the remainder of the year. Meanwhile, direct operating costs per tonne surged to ~$311 vs. ~$260 (Q1-24), impacted by the higher cost of purchasing third-party material for processing, higher royalties and the higher special mining duty, with the Mexican Government adding another 1% special mining duty rate starting January 1st, 2025.

On a positive note, the mine is at least generating positive cash flow given the higher metals prices even if costs continue to creep higher at this mature asset (producing since 1991, but mining in the district dates back to the Spanish in the 1500s).

Looking at Bolañitos, production came in at ~190,500 ounces of silver and ~4,300 ounces of gold, translating to a 53% increase in silver production, offset by a 28% decline in gold production and a significant dip in SEO production year-over-year. Lower production was related to mine sequencing with much lower gold grades (1.43 G/T of gold vs. 1.94 G/T of gold) offset by higher silver grades. Unfortunately, this resulted in a meaningful increase in AISC relative to H2-24 levels despite the record gold price given the impact of lower gold by-product credits, as well as higher royalties, the added special mining duty, offset by more silver ounces sold.

Financial Results

Digging into Endeavour Silver's financial results, they were underwhelming relative to peers, to say the least. Revenue came in at ~$63.5 million (Q1-24: ~$63.7 million) which was down marginally year-over-year and operating cash flow sunk to ~$3.4 million (Q1-24: $4.6 million) despite higher metals prices. The result was a free cash outflow of ~$45 million, mostly affected by the significant decline in silver ounces (~1.22 million vs. ~1.75 million) and gold ounces (~8,500 vs. ~10,900) sold in the period.

Given the continued cash outflows, Endeavour Silver's cash position sunk to ~$65 million, while working capital plunged to ~$15 million vs. ~$79 million heading into 2025. On a positive note, Terronera construction is near completion, and we should see a downtrend in capex going forward. The other good news is that higher silver prices have saved the day to improve the outlook for Q2-25 cash flows and get Terronera past the finish line.

That said, Terronera is going to need to operate as planned, especially given the significant burden of 68,000 ounces of gold hedged, with Endeavour absorbing ~$20 million in net debt from the Kolpa acquisition and having $120 million outstanding on its senior secured debt facility (2031 maturity).

As for its free cash flow profile, the company is finally nearing its inflection point, but the key to materially ramping free cash flow will be Terronera operating as expected. As we can see, Endeavour should generate upwards of $20 million in free cash flow this year after three years of significant free cash outflows, with the potential to generate over $120 million in free cash flow next year if Terronera operates at/above expectations. If all goes to plan, this will help Endeavour Silver join the ranks of other silver producers that have flipped to positive free cash flow, though with some (including Endeavour Silver) resorting to dilutive M&A to help accelerate this process.

Costs and Margins

Looking at costs and margins, Endeavour continues to be a high-cost producer relative to peers, reporting Q1-25 AISC of $24.48/oz, up from $21.44/oz in the year-ago period. This was despite a weaker Mexican Peso that was a tailwind in the period and lower sustaining capital, with costs expected to remain elevated until Terronera is online given that Kolpa is also a higher-cost asset, reporting AISC of $22.80/oz in 2024 and set to see higher sustaining capital spend this year relative to 2024 levels. Hence, while operating cash flow will improve in the upcoming quarter with a new operating asset and higher metals prices, I wouldn't expect to see materially better cost performance, with a negative impact from the reversal in the Mexican Peso that has strengthened vs. the US Dollar (UUP).

Fortunately, AISC margins improved even if AISC has continued to climb, coming in at $7.51/oz (23.5%) vs. $2.03/oz (8.6%) in the year-ago period. These margins are still miles below the industry average, but should improve in 2026 if Terronera operates as expected, with what should be sub $14.50/oz AISC in 2026 (SEO basis) at Terronera even baking in material cost inflation vs. prior estimates.

Recent Developments

Kolpa Acquisition

Endeavour's recently announced acquisition of the Kolpa Mine in Peru closed in early May, with the mine set to contribute more high-cost ounces to Endeavour's Q2-25 results. The good news is that this has allowed for diversification across jurisdictions and brought a longer life asset into the portfolio, especially if Endeavour has some luck adding reserves on this large land package.

On a negative note, the hits keep on coming from a share dilution standpoint, with a $50 million bought deal at US$3.88 to fund the purchase and the issuance of additional shares as part of the cash/share consideration. The result is that Endeavour is now up to ~290 million shares from just ~140 million shares to enter 2020 and while the dilution as part of the most recent deal is excusable on its own for what was a more than reasonable purchase price, it's tough to swallow on top of ~30 million shares issued at US$2.01 under its previous ATM and another ~16 million shares issued at US$4.60 less than a year ago.

Long-Term Growth

In terms of long-term growth, Endeavour Silver's outlook has improved materially, with a new mine set to be brought into commercial production by year-end (Terronera, Mexico) and another already in production joining the portfolio (Kolpa, Peru). The result is that Endeavour should see growth towards 18 million SEOs next year, giving it an industry-leading growth profile vs. 2024 output of ~7.6 million SEOs. However, it's important to note that these two longer-life assets are largely being used to replace and offset lost ounces from Bolañitos/Guanaceví, which are on borrowed time and running out of reserves quickly.

The Guanaceví and Bolañitos mines are the Company's only current sources of mineral production. Current life-of-mine plans provide for a defined production life for mining at the Company's mines. The Company's operating mines have expected lives of two to three years based on current proven and probable reserves, current production levels and management's estimated conversion of resources to reserves."

Consolidated cash costs (excluding Terronera and Kolpa) on a per ounce basis are expected to be higher in 2025 compared to 2024, primarily due to lower silver production from these mines as they enter their final years of production."

- Endeavour Silver Filings

Hence, while we should see a material bump in 2026/2027 production, I would expect much of this growth to be erased by 2028, given that production from Kolpa, even in an expanded scenario (2,500 TPD expansion), will be largely offset by Guanaceví heading offline. Plus, the precious metals contribution from Kolpa vs. Guanaceví is much lower, with Kolpa being a silver-lead-zinc-copper asset vs. Guanaceví as silver/gold. Hence, for those interested in precious metals exposure, per share production and especially per share precious metals production is not making much progress looking out past 2026.

Terronera

As for Terronera, Endeavour initially noted on the approval of construction that the mine would be in production by Q4-24, with commercial production by early to mid-2025 based on its 3-6 month ramp-up timeline. Unfortunately, the mine remains well behind schedule, with the updated outlook being a 90-day ramp-up period from May 1st (implying August 1st), with what will be lower grades fed to the plant to ensure the company is not wasting any high-grade ore as it ensures everything is operating smoothly.

Endeavour expects a 21-month construction period, including 3 to 6 months ramp up to full production with initial production expected in the fourth quarter of 2024."

- Endeavour Silver, April 18th, 2023

Unfortunately, this has meant no update yet to guidance for costs or production at Terronera and with it being over two years now since the last cost update, I wouldn't be surprised to see material cost inflation on both sustaining capital and operating costs at this asset. This isn't a deal-breaker, and Terronera will still offer much lower cost ounces than its current portfolio. Still, it's possible actual costs and updated estimates vs. what investors have been banking on from the 2023 study could be a negative surprise.

The good news is that wet commissioning began in early May (previously guided for "early Q2") and the first slurry was introduced to the concentrate filter. Endeavour noted that preliminary performance was consistent with design expectations. In terms of additional disclosure provided on the call, comments weren't negative, but not particularly upbeat either, and we'll have to wait for the Q2-25 call to see if things are progressing more.

I mean - but before I give it to Don, ultimately, I'd say wet commissioning, it's hard to say how well it's going to date because it's a start-and-stop process. Ultimately, some days, we feel like you're making steps forward. Other days, you take a break. And I think that's just part of the nature of commissioning."

- Endeavour Silver Q1-25 Conference Call

Per Share Growth

Moving to the only metric that matters for precious metals producers, per share growth, Endeavour Silver continues to flail in this department.

Today, the growth of Terronera is being cheered despite the significant cost of bringing this asset online in the form of seemingly never-ending share dilution (~110% share dilution in the past five years). And while this bloating of the share count might be excusable if this industry-leading growth profile was sustainable, the impact of bringing Terronera online behind schedule and over initial budget near the tail end of Guanaceví's life means that the higher production profile will not be sustained, resulting in a brief spike higher in per share metrics followed by a per share production cliff, as highlighted below (yellow line).

Worse, even during this spike, per share metrics are still markedly below 2018 levels, with zero evidence of per share growth here. And as stated in past updates, if a diversified producer cannot hold the line on per share metrics or grow per share metrics, an investor is better off simply holding gold/silver bullion, which will always hold its value vs. shares in producers that have a low regard for their currency and dilute at every turn.

The other significant cost of Terronera that seems to be ignored by the market for this gold-heavy asset is that Endeavour was required to hedge 68,000 ounces of gold or nearly two years of gold production from Terronera at $2,329/oz, meaning that even when this asset finally comes online, a meaningful portion of Endeavour's consolidated gold production is heading out the door at costs over $1,000/oz below spot levels. Hence, even if the steady stream of share dilution is finally in the rearview mirror, the negative effects of achieving this growth will still be felt for another two years, with gold forward settlements occurring from June 2025 to October 2027.

Valuation

Based on ~296 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$5.05, Endeavour Silver trades at a market cap of ~$1.49 billion, making it one of the lowest capitalization producers in the silver space today. This lower market cap relative to peers is largely attributed to its smaller scale (~16 million SEOs in 2026 on precious metals only), its less favorable jurisdiction profile vs. peers like Hecla Mining (HL) and its shorter weighted average reserve life. In fact, two of its current operations are staring down sub 2-year mine lives based on reserves, with just ~380,000 tonnes of material in the reserve category at Bolañitos, or barely 1 year of mine life.

Given the stronger gold/silver price environment and the lower hurdle for reserve conversion, there is certainly room to extend production into H2-26 and potentially further at these two assets. However, it's quite possible that Endeavour Silver could be down to just two assets (albeit stronger ones) by mid-2027, setting up a production cliff within just two years. Given this risk to the production profile and its reduced diversification vs. other investment vehicles that offer silver exposure like Triple Flag (TFPM) and Pan American Silver (PAAS), I think this discount continues to be justified.

So, What's a Fair Value for the Stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 11.0x cash flow and 1.4x P/NAV (8%) for a producer focused in Mexico/Peru (four operating assets in commercial production by 2026) with an inferior track record of per share growth offset by a silver premium, I see a fair value for the stock of US$4.90 (*). This continues to suggest that Endeavour Silver is fully valued at current levels and, given that I require a minimum 35% discount to fair value for Tier-2 focused producers, a significant pullback would be required to make the stock more interesting from a swing-trading standpoint.

(*) The Fair value estimate is based on a 65/35 weighting to P/NAV vs. P/CF. (*)

Obviously, a rising silver price could lift all boats and Endeavour Silver's leverage, industry-leading growth and improved diversification profile certainly make it a superior option to highly concentrated peers on sell signals like Avino Silver & Gold Mines (ASM). Still, I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector and think there are dozens of better options to a serial share diluter with inferior per share metrics like EXK.

Summary

Endeavour Silver saw a sharp decline in production in Q1-25 and while Q2-25 will be better with partial contribution from its new Kolpa Mine in Peru, investors will have to wait until at least Q4-25 to start to see meaningful contribution from Terronera. Looking ahead to 2026, free cash flow will surge, with Endeavour's EV/FCF multiple (2026) dropping closer to ~10x, a more reasonable multiple for a small-cap producer with the bulk of its NAV concentrated in Mexico.

However, this still doesn't offer enough margin of safety, and cheaper options are available elsewhere in the sector, especially when adjusting for overall quality and per share growth metrics. Hence, I don't see any way to justify chasing EXK above US$5.10, and I would view any rallies above US$5.35 before September as an opportunity to book partial profits into strength.

data.title: YMAX: You May Not Get Your Money Back
data.description:
The YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) is an interesting product with attractive elements. I like how it has diversified its holdings so it is a "fund of funds" with less risk in any one stock or sector. The weekly income is also great, as is the 65% dividend. The downside is the consistent erosion in price, but income investors seem to tolerate that.

This article is more focused on one of the strategies I often hear about in the comment section or even in Seeking Alpha articles - with a dividend of 70%, if you hold for a year and a half, you'll have your money returned to you. After that point, you have completely de-risked and can leave your initial investment to pour in dividends. It sounds great, but the strategy has flaws.

YMAX Overview

YMAX is an actively managed ETF that holds around 32 other YieldMax funds at equal weighting.

These funds have a dual objective - to provide income and provide exposure to the underlying shares. They use an options overlay strategy to boost income, but this caps the gains in strong rallies.

YMAX itself doesn't use an options strategy, which makes the 1.28% expense fee high in my opinion. All it does is buy other ETFs and equally weight them. The income these funds provide is then distributed.

The Dividend

The distribution rate is currently listed as 70.28%.

This is based on a simple calculation:

The Distribution Rate is the annual rate an investor would receive if the most recently declared distribution, which includes option income, remained the same going forward. The Distribution Rate is calculated by multiplying an ETF's Distribution per Share by twelve (12), and dividing the resulting amount by the ETF's most recent NAV.

It's not the usual trailing twelve-month calculation, as that can be misleading in funds that have downtrending prices. Generally, as the ETF price drops, the distributions drop too. If you then include the larger distributions from 9 to 12 months ago, they distorted the calculation and make the yield seem higher than it really is. More on this later.

YMAX, therefore, uses the latest distribution and annualizes it, which can also be misleading, as the distributions are variable.

The distribution on 29th May was $0.2149, so the yield would have been around 80%. 4 weeks earlier, the distribution was only $0.1367, so the yield was 55%.

Getting Your Money Back

The variable distributions make it difficult to calculate when an investor will reach their goal and have all their initial investment returned. We could perhaps take an average of recent distributions and then annualize it, but this does not account for the fact that the erosion in NAV is likely to decrease the distributions over time.

A more reliable indication is adding up all the distributions since inception in February 2024. This is $11.4 in total, so with a buy-in point around $20, you will have had 57% of your investment returned in nearly 18 months.

Perhaps February 2024 was a bad time to buy, and YMAX distributions increased when they moved to weekly distributions. This is due to a larger return of capital, but in any case, we can do the same calculation for 2025 YTD. YMAX has distributed a total of $3.9 YTD. That will likely be just over $4.1 at the end of June. If you bought on the 2nd January at $17, you have had 24% of your initial investment returned in around 6 months. In around 2 years, you may have a chance of getting your initial $17 back in distributions.

This doesn't sound too bad, but there is one major caveat. This calculation relies on the distributions staying steady. However, in reality, the NAV is constantly eroding. How can YMAX continue to generate the same income from half the NAV?

NAV Matters

Quite simply, YMAX can't generate the same amount of income from a NAV of 13.5 as it did when the NAV was 20. It can, however, pay the same distribution if it chooses to. Indeed, it can pay double or even triple as it can return as much capital as it wants - there is no limit.
