YMAX has managed to keep the distributions relatively steady even as its NAV has dropped. The recent payment of $0.18 is the same as the first payment of the year when the NAV was around 25% higher.

No matter how much income the fund has, it can top it up to around the same amount with ROC.

This is difficult to track, as the "tax documents" page on the YMAX site does not include a complete month-by-month breakdown of 19a-1 notices. In any case, from a seemingly incomplete list, we can see the June 2024 distribution had 29.95% ROC.

This jumped to 43.98% in October.

And the fund page now states:

The most recent distribution on 06/11/2025 contains 96.28% return of capital and 3.72% income.

So, it appears the ROC is increasing. It also appears the income is decreasing, and 3.72% of the $0.18 distribution means the income was only $0.067. Back in October, it was listed as $0.12.

I would like to track this in more depth, but an SEC search for all the filings from YMAX doesn't provide us more information. In any case, it is logical that more ROC will have to be used to keep distributions constant as the NAV falls.

This is not sustainable. If the funds that YMAX holds were to stay at a steady price, and YMAX continues to pay out around $0.18 a week with 97% ROC, there would be nothing left in less than two years. It's simply math.

Of course, the underlying funds may rally, but any pause or downturn would start to hurt the NAV again. Indeed, YMAX last made a recovery high on May 14th and is fading lower again as the rally in many of the ETFs it holds have slowed.

YMAX probably changes its strategy at some point to stop this NAV erosion. The obvious change would be to lower the distributions, but this would anger investors and could lead to outflows.

Conclusions

Receiving your initial investment returned in distributions is an attractive goal, but I think it's a rosy assumption and an unlikely outcome. Even if you bought YMAX at its launch, you would have 57% returned, and with NAV much lower, the fund's income is not what it was. It may take another year (or two) to reach the goal, by which time the distributions could be considerably lower.

YMAX has managed to keep its distributions consistently high, but has had to use more and more return of capital to do so. Its holdings need to rally strongly for this strategy to be sustainable. If they don't, all the fund's NAV will be returned to the next two years.

data.title: Pursuit Attractions and Hospitality: A Clean Balance Sheet, But A Fully Priced Ticket
data.description:
If you’ve never heard of Pursuit Attractions and Hospitality (PRSU), no worries—this isn’t some brand-new travel startup. We’re actually talking about the company formerly known as Viad Corp. (ring a bell now?). They’ve been a key player in experiential tourism for quite a while.

Actually, their story goes way back.

Right after the Great Depression hit, they started out running intercity buses under the name 'Motor Transit Corporation.' Not long after, they rebranded to 'The Greyhound Corporation,' bringing together around a hundred different bus lines under one roof.

It actually took them about thirty years to branch out into other areas like travel and recreation. By then, they were already using their bus network to cross-sell and drive more ticket sales—a pretty smart move for the time.

But the real turning point came in 1987, when they sold off Greyhound Corporation. That happened just a few years after they bought Glacier Park, Inc. (a former subsidiary of Great Northern Railway), marking a big shift in their focus toward tourism and experiences.

Upon acquisition, Viad began directly operating properties within Glacier National Park in Montana and Waterton Lakes National Park in Alberta, Canada.

The list includes a full package: historic properties like Glacier Park Lodge and Prince of Wales Hotel, as well as more modern ones like Grouse Mountain Lodge.

And that was just the beginning.

Over time, Viad expanded even further via M&A to the point that today they operate across three countries with about fifteen tourist attractions, nearly thirty hotels and lodges, plus convenience stores and restaurants—the kind of setup that, honestly, if my wife finds out about it, she’s booking the whole thing tomorrow.

More recently, the company’s gone through quite a transformation. They sold off their Global Experience Specialists division so they could double down on what they really do best: travel and recreation.

That’s actually when they decided to rebrand from Viad to Pursuit.

After the divestment, Pursuit became a pure-play company in the experiential tourism sector.

Along with that, they adopted the following approach:

  1. With the sale of GES to Truelink Capital late last year, Pursuit has taken a much more conservative approach to capital allocation. What we can see is that in FY 2024 alone they repaid $382 million of long-term debt, taking it to less than $20 million. That’s negative net debt when you consider that they are sitting on $22.8 million of cash;
  2. The logic here is pretty straightforward: when you’ve got a bunch of high-demand attractions in your portfolio, keeping them fresh isn’t just a nice-to-have—it’s a must. Renovations not only keep things looking sharp, they also help drive ROI by boosting guest traffic and, ultimately, revenue. Since FY 2015, Pursuit has grown revenue from its existing properties by 66%, and that momentum doesn’t look like it’s slowing down anytime soon. They’ve already mapped out $200 million in renovation projects to be completed over the next five years—with $38 to $43 million being spent just in FY 2025. That includes things like the 70+ brand-new suites in the Woodland Wing at the Forest Park Hotel in Jasper;
  3. Despite this more focused approach to renewals, that doesn’t mean M&A will go to waste. With the strong balance sheet after the sale of GES, Pursuit has freed up more room to add debt if it wants to—and especially if the right opportunity presents itself. For those who think they’ve stopped, just look at the past year. They’ve added Eddie’s Cafe and Mercantile, Avgar Lookout Retreat and Montana House (properties near their Avgar Village, Montana and within Glacier National Park), and Jasper Sky Tram (in the Canadian Rockies), the latter of which they plan to renovate soon. It seems to me that they’ve become more discerning about acquisitions, prioritizing, among many criteria, an IRR above 15%—which is good.

To keep it simple, here’s how we can group it all:

  • Canadian Rockies: They operate properties in towns such as Banff, Jasper, Waterton and Golden. Pursuit has high-volume attractions ranging from scenic chairlifts (Banff Gondola), boat cruises (Lake Minnewanka Boat Cruise and Maligne Lake Cruise), guided tours and even the highest suspension bridge in Canada (Golden SkyBridge). For lodging, they own the historic Prince of Wales Hotel (Waterton Lakes) and many others;
  • United States: Attractions in several states such as Alaska (Denali, Kenai Fjords and Talkeetna parks), Montana (Glacier National Park, acquired in the 1980s as I said before) and others. Accommodations near the parks and convenience stores and restaurants are also part of the portfolio here;
  • Iceland: They also have strong attractions like FlyOver Iceland (immersive aerial simulation) and the much talked about Sky Lagoon (geothermal spa near Reykjavík).

But even with a full portfolio, it seems that Pursuit didn't perform very well after taking off Viad's clothes and becoming a pure-play.

The stock is actually down 35.7% YTD, which feels a bit strange when you look at the numbers. Since Q1 FY 2024, Pursuit has beaten Wall Street’s revenue estimates in four of the last five quarters—and topped EPS estimates in three of them.

Yes, we all know the broader consumer discretionary space is going through a rough patch (just look at restaurants, retail, casinos), but you've got to wonder: did the market overreact with this sell-off?

To answer that, let's start by taking a look at the numbers.

A Look At The Numbers

Just like I mentioned in my Six Flags (FUN) write-up, Pursuit is a super seasonal business—Q2 and Q3 are where the magic really happens. That said, it doesn’t mean we should ignore Q1 FY 2025. It still gives us some insights, even if it’s not the star of the show.

They reported revenue of $37.6 million for the quarter—a modest increase of about 1% year-over-year.

However, Pursuit saw currency headwinds from the Canadian dollar that impacted approximately $1.3 million. Excluding those impacts, the company would report 4% year-over-year growth.

But what were the drivers of that growth?

The truth is, there was only one: Ticket revenue.

It was $19 million, which represented a 6% year-over-year increase.

The best part is that this growth was driven by both higher ticket prices (up approximately 10% year-over-year, with ETP increasing from $40.28 to $44.11) and visitor volume (459,500 versus 451,800).

The two biggest contributors to this growth in a low season were the brand-new Flyover Chicago (opening in March of last year, boosting attendance over the comparable period) and the expansion of the premium ritual experience at Sky Lagoon (boosting ETP from the premiumization).

Let’s talk about Rooms. Revenue from that segment was down 4% year over year to $7.3 million—but here’s the twist: all the key operating metrics actually improved. RevPAR jumped 9% to $67.56, up from $62.22. ADR rose 7%, and occupancy ticked up too, from 58.2% to 59.2%.

So, while topline sales dipped, the fundamentals underneath tell a much more encouraging story. The real drag here was the lowest room availability at the Forest Park Hotel I mentioned earlier. Once that's back to full capacity, we should see those numbers pick up again.

F&B revenue came in at $6.1 million, down 6% year over year. They didn’t spell it out on the call, but let’s be real, people are being more careful with their spending.

Retail revenue held steady at $1.7 million, flat compared to last year. Meanwhile, Transportation and Others brought in $3.4 million, slipping just 2.8% year over year. Nothing major, but it does show a bit of softness across the board.

After a record $55.6 million in SG&A (up 4.5% year-over-year due to seasonal expenses related to peak season preparation), Pursuit reported EBIT of -$31.3 million, down about $3 million from Q1 FY 2024. That gap widened to $6 million after the GES sale, leading the company to report a net loss of $31.1 million for the quarter.

That translated into OCF of -$24.4 million (up from -$7.5 million in Q1 FY 2024), and after Capex of $9.9 million, Pursuit reported FCF of -$34.3 million. They partially offset that by netting just under $5 million in debt, but by the end of the quarter they were left with a cash deficit of nearly $30 million.

But as I said earlier, Q1 isn’t exactly the best lens to see what’s coming next.

What really matters is the guidance—and yes, they did go ahead and reaffirm their outlook for FY 2025. So let’s dig into that a bit more.

They reported the following:

  • They are expecting double-digit annual revenue growth. Considering that they reported $366.5 million in revenue in FY 2024, we can estimate somewhere between $403.1 and $440 million (10% to 20% year-over-year revenue growth). Wall St. is targeting $408 million, which is also within the range I estimated;
  • A 27% to 40% year-over-year growth in adjusted EBITDA, targeting somewhere between $98 to $108 million. If in FY 2024 EBITDA was $61.7 million (about 80% of adjusted EBITDA), we can expect an EBITDA between $78.4 to $86.4 million for FY 2025;
  • Although not explicitly stated in the guidance, Wall St. sees a consensus EPS of $0.92 for FY 2025.

They’re counting on this growth coming from a few key things: Jasper National Park bouncing back, the new additions they made late last year (like Eddie’s Cafe and Mercantile, Avgar Lookout Retreat, Montana House, and Jasper Sky Tram), steady improvements in room performance, and ongoing renovations.

Pursuit Is A 'Buy' Or Are You Buying the Wrong Ticket?

Let’s see if Pursuit is actually a bargain after dropping over 35% so far this year.

I’m kicking things off with a Comps Model.

Projecting an EV of $956 million for FY 2025 and revenue ranges between $403.1 to $440 million, we would have an EV/S between 2.1x to 2.3x.

Comparing this to other comparable companies (please note that it is very difficult to find highly purchasable companies, so I will use other companies in the attractions and hospitality sector), we would have the following picture:

  • Vail Resorts (MTN): EV/S of 2.9x;
  • Carnival Corporation (CCL): EV/S of 2.2x;
  • Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE): EV/S of 3x;
  • United Parks & Resorts (PRKS): EV/S of 2.6x;
  • Six Flags Entertainment: EV/S of 2.5x;
  • Dave & Buster's Entertainment (PLAY): EV/S of 2.1x.

The average EV/S of comparable companies is 2.5x. Adjusting for Pursuit (FY 2025 midpoint revenue of $421.5 million, net debt of -$5.7 million, and shares outstanding of 28.2 million), we get a price target of $37.50.

This would give us an upside of approximately 36% from here.

But of course, this isn’t the only angle we can look at. With the same projected EV and a midpoint EBITDA of $82.4 million, we would arrive at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.6x. This is higher than the industry median multiple of 9.9x.

Averaging across the control group, we would arrive at an average EV/EBITDA of 9.4x. Using this multiple in our model and plugging in the projections for Pursuit, we would arrive at a price target of $27.50.

That is roughly where the stock is trading today.

If you go by what Wall Street’s saying, it looks like they’re focused on EV/Sales, with a consensus price target hovering around $40.

I'm not quite on the same page. I think EV/EBITDA paints a more realistic picture here, and based on that, the stock looks more fairly valued right now. That said, if we split the difference between the two models, we land at a price target of about $32.50—still an upside of roughly 18%. However, that drops to $30 when you consider Wall St.'s revenue forecasts.

But here's the thing: none of that really excites me. And I'll tell you why.

Pursuit has major renovations lined up, which means heavy Capex ahead. That’s going to weigh on free cash flow. On top of that, it’s a seasonal business and leans heavily on the consumer discretionary cycle—both of which add risk.

So, even with a potential 18% upside, it’s not exactly compelling. The stock isn't dirt-cheap, and without dividends or buybacks to sweeten the wait, it just doesn't strike me as the kind of place I want to park my money right now.

That said, the stock’s not overpriced either. It’s trading more or less where it should be, which is why I’m landing on a 'Hold' rating for now.

If we pencil in a price target around $30, we’re looking at a margin of safety of less than 10%. Not quite enough to get excited about, but also not a reason to sell yet.

data.title: MP Materials: Vertical Integration Unlocks Value In A Volatile Rare Earth Market
data.description:
Introduction:

In today's article, I will be looking at MP Materials (NYSE:MP), a company that has been largely under covered for years up until recently when it burst onto the scene of many investors. I, however, have been following this company since its IPO and have grown a strong interest into this story, it's management team, and the global potential for the rare earth industry. Unlike most of the companies I follow, this is a small-cap company that has historically been extremely difficult to construct a proper valuation on. Since its initial public offering in 2017, this company has accomplished more than I think anyone expected, and has delivered exceptional operational advancements and I believe is nearing the inflection point from a small mining focused company to a three segment, fully integrated monopoly controlling the American rare earth industry that is capable of disrupting its global markets. In turn, setting a new standard as the low-cost producer of refined rare earth metals and the highest grade NDPR magnets. Today I will be covering where the company currently is operationally, where the global market is, it's superb and visionary management team, as well I will be placing an initial valuation on the company. My goal is to allow readers to better understand why this company has gained national attention as of recent and where it can go in 2025 and beyond. In this article, I rate MP Materials a strong hold after its recent YTD price appreciation, with an ideal buy zone in the $26–$28 range. I would not encourage buying this stock at these current prices, but for those interested in the company, I encourage you to stay on the sidelines and follow along as the story continues to unfold.

Background:

MP Materials was founded in 2017 after acquiring the Mountain Pass mine in California. This site is the only rare earth mining and processing facility of scale in the United States. Since then, MP has steadily evolved from a niche mining company into a vertically integrated player across the rare earth supply chain. While global attention often centers on China’s control of rare earths, MP has quietly built the foundation to emerge as a strategic counterweight.

The company began with upstream production, selling rare earth concentrate to partners abroad. Over time, the company has reinvested those profits to expand downstream production efforts. Today MP not only operates the Mountain Pass mine but is also ramping up refining and magnet production at its new facility in Fort Worth, Texas in 2025 and expects full operations in 2026. This transition marks a turning point in the company’s business model. MP aims to become the first domestic producer of both separated rare earth oxides and finished NdFeB magnets, which are used in electric vehicles, defense technologies, and advanced manufacturing.

MP is led by CEO James Litinsky, who has guided the company with a long-term vision after rebuilding the mine from former ownership's bankruptcy. He has created a company of operational excellence while navigating through extremely volatile mineral markets along with predatory pricing tactics by global competitors. In under a decade since the reopening of the mine, he will have built out a three segment business operating in multiple states. What has impressed me the most is balance sheet management by CFO Ryan Corbett. Financing the growth and expansion MP has experienced since 2017 while still maintaining only a ~100 million net debt position is no easy task. It's rare to find a high-growth company that pairs its expansion strategy with such a well-managed balance sheet. As an investor, that combination is both compelling and reassuring.

Global Market and Rare Earth Demand:

The rare earth market sits at the crossroad of the AI revolution, energy transition, national security, and dozens of downstream industries dependent on these critical metals. Among the 17 rare earth elements, neodymium (Nd) and praseodymium (Pr), combined as NdPr oxide, have become the most economically critical due to their use in ultra-high strength permanent magnets. These power electric vehicle drivetrains, wind turbines, robotics, medical devices, defense systems, etc. Without these critical metals, the advancement of EV markets, robotics, physical AI applications, and cutting-edge defense and aerospace technologies would not be possible.

In a recent mineral report from Adamas Intelligence, they expect NdFeB magnet demand is expected to grow at an 8.7% compounded annual growth rate over the next decade, brought forward by double-digit growth for robotics, air mobility and electric vehicle sectors, which will also translate to comparable demand growth for the rare earth elements (i.e., Neodymium, dysprosium and praseodymium) these magnets contain. Industrial applications, wind turbines, and consumer electronics are also driving usage higher. According to Argus Research Group, global demand for neodymium and praseodymium is set to outstrip supply through the early 2030s, with significant shortages expected unless new refining and magnet production capacity is developed outside China. This looming global supply and demand imbalance has been highlighted by MP Materials on multiple earnings calls.

Today, China controls over 85% of rare earth refining and more than 90% of global magnet production. China has historically been accused of weaponizing access to rare earths and therefore has the ability to create supply disruptions which would have ripple effects across energy, defense, and industrial sectors globally and in the United States. MP Materials is currently the only scaled end to end rare earth producer in North America and is one of just a handful of companies globally working to close the processing and magnet manufacturing gap.

What makes MP especially compelling is the scope of its vision. Rather than remaining a raw material exporter, the company is executing a three-stage model. Mining and beneficiation to then oxide separation on-site at Mountain Pass, California, and downstream magnet manufacturing at its new "Independence" Facility in Texas. Once ramped up to projected full run rate, MP is projected to produce 6,075 tonnes of NdPr oxide annually, which will be more than 9% of expected global supply. And while the Fort Worth plant will initially consume less than 10% of that total to produce 1,000 tonnes of high-grade NdFeB magnets, the company has built the independence facility to be able to scale this downstream capacity further over time. Which I fully expect for them to announce expansion projects with new foundational customers in the forthcoming years at the production facility.

With rare earth prices stabilizing after a post-2022 correction, I expect an upward trend from a bottom earlier this year. As well as foundational North American customers like General Motors already under contract, MP’s vertical integration positions it to capture value across the supply chain. Importantly, this isn’t just a commodity story. It’s a platform bet on American industrial resilience, and MP is building the backbone that no one else has been able to.

Where MP Stands Today:

2025 and 2026 are shaping up to be the most important years in MP Materials short history. After nearly a decade of planning, reinvestment, and infrastructure build out, the company has finally arrived at a turning point before the full realization of its three-phase strategy: mining, refining, and magnet production. This transformation comes at a pivotal geopolitical moment. In response to China's retaliatory tariffs, export controls, as well as price manipulation, MP made the bold move to stop all rare earth concentrate shipments to China, which represents a major share of revenue through sales to Shenghe Resources. Rather than sell into a market under 125% tariffs, the company is stockpiling material onsite and continuing to accelerate downstream operations.

The company’s California facility at Mountain Pass is now processing nearly half of its upstream production into separated rare earth oxides, particularly NdPr. This material is now being sold into more strategic markets including Japan, South Korea, and the U.S., which were previously inaccessible without downstream refining. MP has worked to broaden its customer base beyond raw material buyers to include industrial and manufacturing end-users who depend on refined inputs, which previously only have been supplied by China.

Later this year, MP expects to deliver its first batch of U.S. made NdFeB magnets to General Motors from its new Fort Worth “Independence” facility. This will mark the first time in decades that American mined and refined rare earths are used to produce high-performance magnets domestically at scale. This is an amazing accomplishment by the company and is one to watch out for later this year. Once the upstream mine & downstream facility are fully ramped, MP projects an annual run rate of 60,000 metric tons of rare earth oxide (REO) concentrate, 6,075 metric tons of NdPr oxide, and 1,000 metric tons of finished NdFeB magnets. While only ~10% of the company’s output will be used for magnet production, the strategic value of capturing every stage of the value chain cannot be overstated. This creates wonderful synergy and recycling opportunities not available to other producers globally. Giving them the ability to become one of the world's lowest cost producers. This is the moment MP Materials has been preparing for since day one, and is beginning to execute on everything it has once promised.

Valuation: Modeling MP Materials at Full Run Rate

To evaluate MP Materials’ mid to long-term potential, I modeled what the company could look like once all three phases of its vertically integrated strategy are fully scaled, which I believe to be possible in 2026 and is fully expected in 2027 under normal circumstances. That includes steady-state production from the Mountain Pass mine, full oxide separation in California, and commercial magnet output from its Fort Worth facility and full deliveries to General Motors. Using realistic production targets and expected mineral pricing, this approach reflects a grounded scenario that MP is actively building toward.

COO Michael Rosenthal has guided to annual production of 60,000 Metric tons mined at Mountain Pass. From this 60,000, 5,400 metric tons of NdPr oxide and 35,000 metric tons of rare earth oxide (REO) concentrate will be mined and refined, and 1,000 metric tons of NdFeB magnets will be manufactured at the independence facility. Using market pricing forecast of $65/kg for NdPr, $6,000/MT for REO, and $70/kg for high-grade magnets, this translates into total revenue of $630 million.

Product Volume Price Conversion Factor Revenue
NdPr (MT) 5,400 $65/Kg 1,000 $350 M
REO (MT) 35,000 $6,000/MT 1 $210 M
NdFeB Magnets (MT) 1,000 $70/KT 1,000 $70 M

Broken out, NdPr oxide would contribute $351 million, REO concentrate $210 million, and magnets $70 million. These assumptions align with MP’s announced capacity goals and publicly stated ratios of downstream supply mix derived upstream mining.

From the total revenue of $630 million, I will apply a 35% EBITDA margin of total revenue which, I believe, is completely achievable with higher and more stable commodity prices, continued synergies, material recycling opportunities, and advanced refining technologies, etc. This margin would yield $220.85 million in total EBITDA. After this, I then applied a 25x EV/EBITDA multiple, which reflects MP’s unique geopolitical positioning, monopoly U.S. supply control, and national security relevance which has achieved national recognition and support from the Trump Administration. This is also consistent with other rare earth companies' current or expected multiples, which range from a 15–35 EV/EBITDA. Using a 25x, this results in an enterprise value of $4.417 billion. After subtracting $100 million in net debt, the implied equity value is $4.317 billion. With 163.76 million shares outstanding, the fair value per share lands at $33.11 with 2026 EBITDA forecast.

To help frame the potential range of outcomes, I ran a sensitivity analysis with 20% downside and upside cases on commodity and magnet pricing.

Case Revenue EBITDA EV (25x) Equity Value Share Price
Low $504.8M $176.7M $4.417B $4.317B $26.36
Base $631.0M $220.9M $5.522B $5.422B $33.11
High $757.2M $264.0M $6.600B $6.500B $39.69

With the recent large run up in share price of this company over the past few months, I rate MP a hold rating based on my valuation analysis. If it dips down into the $26 I will add on more and continue my position into the company as I do believe this company will grow to many multiples higher as they grow end use magnet production in the coming years with new foundational customers passed 2026.

Risk to Thesis:

One of the most obvious risks to the MP Materials thesis is its natural exposure to commodity price volatility in an illiquid market, particularly neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) oxide. NdPr is the corner piece of MP’s products, and in 2024 the market did experience a sharp drop in realized pricing from $70/kg in 2023 down to $51/kg. This decline meaningfully impacted revenue and margins, and future fluctuations could create similar threats. These pricing swings are influenced by a host of uncontrollable external variables including interest rates, exchange rates, tariffs, and supply dynamics within China, which, as stated before, controls over 85% of global refining and more than 90% of magnet manufacturing. While MP’s move into refining and magnets will mitigate some price sensitivity by capturing more margin per unit mined, commodity pricing risk remains a vulnerability.

Another material concern stems from geopolitical instability and the company’s reliance on a majority buyer, Shenghe Resources of China, for the majority of its concentrate sales. Following retaliatory tariffs and export controls in early 2025, MP suspended exports to Shenghe and instead announced to stockpile concentrate and prioritize downstream integration ramp up. While this was a strategically aligned move in my opinion, it temporarily cuts off a major revenue stream and accelerates MP’s dependence on ramping up oxide and magnet sales. I will be very eager and curious to see next quarter earnings and expected announcements to see how they have continued to navigate this problem and how it has impacted their bottom line. With any delays in bringing downstream production capabilities online or signing new customer agreements, this problem could prolong MP's revenue streams, especially if macro headwinds persist or if competition from China intensifies as negotiations continue.

Finally, MP’s operational execution remains a key risk. The company is in the midst of building the first fully scaled U.S. rare earth supply chain in decades, which is a task that is both capital intensive and extremely complex with no previous western hemisphere model to follow. Delays in commissioning the Independence magnet facility, difficulty in maintaining consistent quality, or challenges in securing skilled labor or equipment could all affect MP’s ability to fulfill its long-term supply agreement. The company has outlined ambitious goals of reaching 6,075 MT of NdPr oxide production and 1,000 MT of NdFeB magnet output, but any underperformance in achieving these run rates would undercut the high valuation multiple investors may assign to full vertical integration. Additionally, MP's ability to convert new customer discussions into firm long-term offtake contracts remains unproven at scale other than the one foundational customer. In short, while the strategic upside is substantial, the execution path remains complex and subject to multiple industry and geopolitical headwinds which, in part, may be out of their control.

Final Thoughts:

As of mid-June 2025, MP Materials has had a successful year in the stock market. Currently, the company stands up over 100% year to date.

Operationally, in the first quarter, the company showed real progress. It’s now producing separated rare earth oxides at Mountain Pass in meaningful volumes, and it remains on track to begin delivering its first batch of domestically produced magnets to General Motors later this year. That’s a milestone worth paying attention to.

Looking at the broader picture, I continue to believe that MP is one of the most strategically important companies in America today. Rare earths are no longer just a commodity story. They’re tied directly to national defense, clean energy, advanced manufacturing, and future technologies. With that in mind, it’s hard to ignore the potential for government support in the years ahead, especially as this has been highlighted as a choke point in the United States and Chinese active trade negotiations. With President Trump announcing potential for government support in continuing to build out the rare earth magnet industry domestically. MP is in the best position to benefit if that happens. They’re building the exact type of domestic supply chain the government wants to see more of.

Now, let’s talk about the stock. It’s had a big run recently. After a strong year-to-date move, I think a lot of near-term optimism is already priced in, which can be noted from my valuation section above. That’s why I’m rating MP as a strong hold. I still believe in the long-term upside. I still think this company can become the backbone of a rare earth supply chain in North America. Personally, I would feel more comfortable adding to my position if it pulls back into the $26 to $28 range. For investors who don’t own it, yet, I’d suggest keeping it on your radar and watching closely over the next few quarters. This story is just getting started, and what happens in the back half of 2025 could be incredibly important.

data.title: JEPI: Flat Markets Would Make Them Outperform, But I Don't See That Happening
data.description:
With $40.02 billion in assets under management, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (NYSEARCA:JEPI) is the largest ETF offered by JPMorgan (JPM). This ETF is an alternative to the JPMorgan Equity Income Mutual Fund (OIEIX), differing in that it utilizes derivative products to enhance the fund's yield. Currently, the ETF has a trailing dividend yield of 8.36%, and in my opinion, it is not worth initiating a position in it as flat markets are what really benefit their structured product, and therefore, in this analysis, I will rate the ETF as a "sell."

JEPI: A Massive Success for JPMorgan Asset Management

First, I would like to point out that JEPI is a relatively new product launched in 2020, and so far, it has been a success for JPMorgan Asset Management. As shown in the image above, monthly net flows have remained consistently positive since inception. Additionally, as illustrated in the image below, the AUM has continued to rise in an upward trend, despite paying high dividend yields that decrease the assets under management.

This rise in popularity benefits investors with lower implicit fees from bid-ask spreads, as higher volume in a particular security narrows the spread. Currently, over the past three months, the ETF has had an average daily volume of $290.23 million, and generally exhibits a pretty narrow bid-ask spread of 0.02%. Based on an annual expense fee of 0.35%, if investors buy the ETF and hold it for one year, they will be paying a one-year fee of 0.37%, which is attractive considering that the ETF is actively managed and has a team of portfolio managers and analysts around.

Investment Mandate

The ETF has a mandate that requires it to invest at least 80% of its assets in equities, with a tilt towards value large-cap companies in the S&P 500 that pay high dividends and are more defensive during economic downturns. The following 20% is utilized to sell call options through S&P 500 equity-linked notes, thereby generating an income.

The drawback of that 20%, utilized to follow a covered call strategy, is that by selling calls, the position will incur losses if the S&P 500 price rises. Of course, these losses are partly covered by an equity portfolio that has a beta close to one. However, if the S&P 500 drops, it would be beneficial to the derivative position, as income would be gained.

Although the ETF still has a massive exposure to beta, its structured composition allows it to be less volatile than the S&P 500, and therefore, it becomes a positive characteristic for income investors in retirement or for investors who seek a correlation of less than one to the equity markets.

Yield Difference in a Structured ETF vs Classical Income ETF

Fund JPMorgan Equity Premium Income JEPI JPMorgan Equity Income (OIEIX) Vanguard High Dividend Yield (VYM) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity (SCHD) iShares Select Dividend (DVY)
4-Year Average Yield

8.69%

3.57%

2.95%

3.38%

3.48%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Although there are many popular dividend ETFs, such as the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, and the iShares Select Dividend ETF, the difference in yield between a classical one and one that sells equity-linked notes ((ELNs)) could be considerable. For example, at an average yield of 8.69%, JEPI's yield exceeds the rest, with much lower averages below 3.6%.

Simultaneously, during the past five years, the price return of the JEPI has been pretty mediocre at 10.11%, which implies a poor annual rate of 1.94%. Nonetheless, the total return was much higher at 70.29%, but as expected, it underperformed the S&P 500 by 36.53%.

Potential JEPI's Performance During a Bull, Flat, or Bear Market

In my opinion, this is not an ETF for long-term holding, unless the investor is seeking reduced volatility and wants to enhance their yield while maintaining exposure to beta. Also, if an investor is willing to do this, this is a product that is highly tax inefficient. First, it is actively managed, so it increases asset turnover, and second, gains in derivatives would likely be considered short-term capital gains.

Meanwhile, I believe that this is an ETF more suitable for tactical opportunities. For example, when markets are expected to be flat, this ETF is likely to outperform the S&P 500, as gains will occur on the side of the derivative, and higher dividends from a tilt in value companies will outperform growth stocks.

Simultaneously, during a classical economic recession (not the one seen in Covid, where Tech outperformed), I believe the ETF would slightly outperform the S&P 500, again, due to the higher concentration of defensive companies, and due to the effects of the embedded derivative. Nonetheless, this gain from the covered call positions is limited, and therefore, I believe the outperformance would be only slight. Also, if there is a V-shape recovery, the gains from the derivatives won't be that substantial, and in return, there won't be a huge difference.

Last, not everything could be great, and if there is a trendy market to the upside, as it's the most normal condition in US equity markets, the ETF will underperform, providing investors with much less returns than holding a pure income ETF or the S&P 500.

JEPI: Perhaps Not A Great Alternative During The Current Landscape

As commented, what could potentially benefit JEPI and make it outperform the S&P 500 are mainly flat markets, with a slight gain during bear markets.

Although equity markets haven't exhibited large price changes over the past five weeks, there are several scenarios that could trigger a directional shift in the market again. Mid-term, it is hard to believe that markets will continue to be flat, especially with all the tariff concerns, negotiations that need to occur, geopolitical tensions, rising oil prices, and more scenarios that could cause markets to trend if situations improve or deteriorate.

Although this ETF could slightly outperform during bear markets, I believe the current landscape makes it different. This is because a potential economic impact from the effect of tariffs would most likely affect value stocks that are required to import from abroad. Potentially generating a divergence between value and growth stocks, creating another anomaly, as seen during the pandemic.

Therefore, considering all these factors, I do not consider the current landscape a great tactical opportunity for this ETF; thus, I will assign it a "sell rating."
